variation.emphasis: Lead with the strongest legal-sufficiency point and keep it tight.

advocate_args: no facts on conditions precedent or damages

transcript.advocate_opening:
Your Honor, the Complaint fails at the threshold because it never pleads a plausible breach-of-contract claim under Rule 12(b)(6). Even taking its factual allegations as true, Plaintiff does not identify the contractual provision supposedly breached, does not allege facts showing satisfaction of any condition precedent, and does not plead concrete damages. That is not a technical defect; it is a failure to allege the essential elements of the claim. Under Twombly and Iqbal, a plaintiff must do more than recite “there was a contract, it was breached, and I was harmed.” Without tethering the claim to an actual contractual obligation and facts showing entitlement to performance, the Court cannot infer liability in any non-conclusory way.

The fraud claim is even more deficient. Rule 9(b) requires the who, what, when, where, and how of the alleged misrepresentation. The Complaint provides none of that. It does not identify who made any statement, when or where it was made, how it was communicated, or what makes it false beyond hindsight accusation. And scienter is pled only by attaching labels like “knowing” and “intentional,” which is precisely the sort of conclusory pleading Rule 9(b) rejects. The use of undifferentiated group pleading against all Defendants only underscores the defect; particularity cannot be satisfied by lumping everyone together.

Dismissal should therefore be complete, and the fraud claim should be dismissed with prejudice. Plaintiff has already amended once, yet the pleading still lacks the core factual material required by Rules 8 and 9(b). On this record, the deficiencies are substantive, not cosmetic.

ARGUMENTS: No specific contractual breach; No facts on conditions precedent or damages; Fraud not pled with particularity; Dismiss fraud with prejudice
transcript.opponent_attack:
The motion fails at the threshold because it demands pleading detail the Rules do not require.

On the contract claim, Defendants’ centerpiece objection—that Plaintiff did not “identify the specific contractual provision breached”—is not a Rule 12(b)(6) rule. Rule 8 requires a plausible claim, not a contract-law treatise in the complaint. A breach claim is adequately pleaded by alleging the existence of a contract, Plaintiff’s performance (or excuse), Defendants’ breach, and resulting damages. That is especially true where the complaint identifies the agreement and the conduct constituting the breach; a plaintiff need not quote the exact clause number to survive dismissal. Defendants’ conditions-precedent argument is even weaker: Rule 9(c) expressly permits conditions precedent to be alleged generally. They cannot convert a rule that relaxes pleading into a basis for dismissal. And their damages argument misstates the standard again—damages must be plausibly alleged, not liquidated or proven with precision at the pleading stage.

The fraud argument is no better. Rule 9(b) requires particularity sufficient to give notice of the misconduct charged, not pre-discovery access to every internal detail Defendants now say is missing. If the complaint identifies the fraudulent transaction or scheme, the substance of the misrepresentations, the approximate timeframe, and the role each Defendant played, that is enough to put Defendants on notice and permit a response. The motion’s attack on scienter is likewise overstated: malice, intent, knowledge, and other conditions of mind may be alleged generally. Defendants try to impose a heightened scienter pleading standard beyond the Rule’s text.

Most importantly, the relief requested is unsustainable. Even if the Court finds any aspect of the fraud allegations wanting, that is a classic Rule 9(b) repleading issue—not grounds for dismissal with prejudice after only one amendment. The Ninth Circuit and the Federal Rules’ liberal amendment policy strongly favor leave to amend absent futility, and Defendants show none. Their “substance rather than form” label is just conclusion, not argument.

Bottom line: the motion is built on heightened pleading demands the Rules reject. At most, Defendants have identified arguable requests for greater detail, not a basis to terminate the case.

ATTACK: Rules 8/9(c)/15 defeat hypertechnical dismissal
transcript.bench_pressure_points: Counsel, your motion is strongest if the Complaint truly fails element by element, but right now you are arguing at a high level of abstraction. On the contract claim, I want you to tell me precisely why the allegations identifying the agreement and the conduct said to violate it are insufficient as a matter of law, particularly in light of Rule 9(c)’s allowance that conditions precedent may be pleaded generally. And on fraud, do not just recite “who, what, when, where, how”; point me to what the Complaint actually alleges and why those allegations fail to give fair notice as to each defendant, as opposed to merely lacking the level of detail you would prefer before discovery.
transcript.advocate_rebuttal:
We are not asking for a clause number; we are pointing out that the Complaint never pleads the operative contractual promise at all—only the legal conclusion that “Defendants breached”—so neither the Court nor Defendants can tell what duty arose under the identified agreement, what conduct violated that duty, or whether the alleged breach is even cognizable. Rule 9(c) allows conditions precedent to be pleaded generally, but it does not excuse pleading them altogether: the Complaint alleges no facts, even generally, that Plaintiff performed or that any prerequisite to Defendants’ obligation occurred, and it compounds that defect by alleging damages only as abstract harm untethered to any identified breach.  

On fraud, this is not a demand for evidentiary detail before discovery; it is a notice problem on the face of the pleading, because the Complaint does not identify which defendant said what to Plaintiff, when, where, or by what means, and instead relies on impermissible group allegations plus bare assertions that the conduct was “knowing” and “intentional.” That leaves each defendant without fair notice of the particular misrepresentation and role attributed to him or it, which is exactly what Rule 9(b) forbids.
transcript.bench_final:
Granted, at least in substantial part. On the contract claim, the problem is not the absence of a clause number but the absence of any pleaded contractual obligation that would let the Court assess breach, performance or excuse, and resulting damages; and the fraud allegations, as described, appear deficient under Rule 9(b) because they rely on undifferentiated group pleading rather than the who-what-when-where of the alleged misrepresentation.

DECISIVE: Movant’s rebuttal that the Complaint never pleads the operative contractual promise at all—only the conclusion that Defendants “breached.”
